text: The robotic surgical setup includes a cadia instrument in arm two and monopolar scissors or shears in arm three, prepared for use in the procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the pulmonary fissure using robotic instruments, separating the lobes of the lung.
The camera view shows the middle lobe being gently touched, with the lower lobe visible at the bottom of the frame and the upper lobe seen in the distance.
The upper lobe is visually observed with minor adhesions to the chest wall, which are not being addressed during the procedure.
The surgeon exposes the pulmonary artery located just below the middle lobe, revealing the vessel's position relative to the surrounding lung tissue.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the fissure, providing clear visualization and control of the pulmonary artery, which is prominently exposed beneath the middle lobe.
The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to dissect tissue, likely around the pulmonary artery, as part of the lobectomy procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the anterior oblique fissure, a thin tissue plane separating the middle and lower lobes of the lung, providing clear exposure of the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon exposes the middle lobe vein beneath the completed anterior oblique fissure, providing a clear view of the vein's location relative to the surrounding lung tissue.
The surgeon identifies the middle lobe vein anteriorly, followed by the middle lobe bronchus located directly behind it.
The surgeon identifies one or two pulmonary artery segmental vessels located behind the middle lobe bronchus, preparing to address these vessels before completing the fissure dissection.
The surgical field is viewed through the Intuitive Robot's camera, positioned centrally and low, providing an 8x magnified, 3D view. The phrenic nerve is visible to the right of the surgeon's shears.

The surgeon exposes the middle lobe vein and identifies a lymph node at station R-eleven I, located between the middle and lower lobes. The lymph node is carefully lifted and removed en bloc with meticulous handling.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully remove the lymph node located between the middle and lower lobes of the lung, ensuring complete extraction.
The surgeon carefully excises the lymph node en bloc, ensuring complete removal while maintaining the integrity of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully removes the R-eleven lymph node, emphasizing the importance of en bloc resection, particularly in challenging areas like station seven and the two-R, four-R block.

The surgeon provides a clear view of the basal pulmonary artery and the middle lobe bronchus, visible just below the surgical shears. The middle lobe is positioned above. The surgeon carefully avoids cutting through a lymph node, maintaining clear tissue planes and anatomical boundaries around the pulmonary artery and bronchus.

The surgeon's assistant introduces a peanut dissector to remove the R-Eleven lymph node through an anterior lower utility incision. The camera is positioned in the eighth rib space, alongside the Cadillac and scissors, while a slightly lower anterior utility incision accommodates a fourth port for the third arm. An XI system may use another cadier for lobe traction if needed.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to identify anatomical structures within the chest cavity. Peanuts or swabs are kept nearby to manage any bleeding by applying pressure.
The surgeon continues dissection around the pulmonary vein, providing a clear view of the middle lobe pulmonary vein.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection with a peanut and monopolar shears to identify the upper border between the middle lobe and the upper lobe pulmonary vein.
The surgeon begins dissection anteriorly between the bronchus and the pulmonary vein, using precise techniques to separate these structures.
The surgeon achieves a clear view of the middle lobe pulmonary vein without needing to flip the middle lobe posteriorly, maintaining the current orientation for optimal visualization.
Tristan adjusts the surgical instruments, placing the Maryland forceps in port arm two and the Acadia in port arm four, facilitating the blunt dissection around the pulmonary vein.

The surgeon maintains focus on the middle lobe bronchus while observing the vein for any bulging or bowing. Using the Maryland forceps, the surgeon carefully maneuvers the instrument, ensuring the tips are visible, and proceeds to separate tissue layers with precision, utilizing the visual feedback provided by the robotic system.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the robotic instruments, ensuring the tips are visible before separating tissue around a vein, demonstrating a technique applicable to both veins and arteries.
The surgeon has successfully looped around and isolated the vein, lifting it up to prepare for the application of a surgical stapler.
The surgical team swaps out the second robotic arm to bring in the stapler, utilizing a three-arm technique for this straightforward pulmonary lobectomy procedure.

The surgeon introduces the intuitive stapler, a white 30, through the posterior port to staple the targeted structures. The stapler is maneuvered around the bronchus with articulation to achieve proper positioning.
The surgeon has exposed the bronchus while isolating the vein, providing a clear view of these structures.
The surgeon performs a final inspection around the bronchus, which is clearly visible due to optimal camera positioning, while exposing the border adjacent to the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon visually identifies the middle lobe pulmonary arteries, noting their variable anatomy, with the medial and lateral arteries labeled A4 and A5. The arteries are positioned above the Maryland dissector, while the bronchus is visible below.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate anatomical structures, carefully observing the anatomy to ensure no hitching occurs during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a blunt cadia instrument anteriorly and a Maryland dissector posteriorly, ensuring safe manipulation around the superior pulmonary vein to the upper lobe. Careful attention is given to avoid accidental pressure on the pulmonary artery.

The surgeon uses a blunt cadia to navigate around the back of the middle lobe bronchus, observing tissue movement. The robotic approach provides a down-up view from the bottom of the chest, allowing visualization of both sides of a vessel.
The surgeon addresses minor bleeding from the middle lobe by applying electrocautery to control the bleeding in real-time.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers a blunt cadier around the pulmonary artery, ensuring safe passage without causing damage. The instrument is then positioned around the middle lobe bronchus, preparing for a precise stapling when needed.

The surgeon prepares to fire the stapler around the middle lobe bronchus, with the Maryland and Cadier instruments in place. The artery is safely slung, and the surgeon performs a brief dissection around it before mimicking the firing action.

The surgeon uses a peanut dissector to explore the chest cavity, focusing on identifying the pulmonary arteries. The upper lobe shows a few adhesions, which are not addressed. The A-four artery, the lateral segmental artery to the middle lobe, is clearly visible within a well-defined horizontal fissure.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue around the pulmonary artery, ensuring no hitching occurs, and successfully visualizes the A5 artery in the distance. The use of a Maryland and Cadier instrument allows for safe navigation around the vessel without causing damage.

The surgeon uses a vessel loop to encircle the pulmonary artery, ensuring control and safety for subsequent stapling. The robotic camera is adjusted to zoom in and out, providing a clear view of the vessel manipulation.
The surgeon uses a Cavidian instrument to maneuver around the pulmonary vessels, employing a leader catheter for precise navigation.
The surgeon removes the Maryland dissector and inserts a white 30 load stapler into the surgical field, preparing for vessel stapling with the Xi robotic system.

The surgeon uses the white thirty load stapler to fire on the lateral middle lobe segmental artery, with the screen indicating the blade's advancement and retraction. The stapler is then retracted, providing a clear view of the A-V medial segmental artery and the pulmonary artery, with a well-defined fissure visible.
The surgeon confidently maneuvers through the surgical field, ensuring safety while passing near the anterior segmental artery to the upper lobe.

The surgeon passes a vessel loop around the pulmonary artery and introduces a white thirty vascular load stapler. The stapler is advanced in a straight shot without much articulation, providing precise control over the stapling process.
The surgeon maneuvers around the bronchus, having completed the dissection of arteries and veins. The remaining task is to address the bronchus and the easily accessible fissure.
The surgeon places a vessel loop around the middle lobe bronchus, preparing to fire the stapler. The remaining task is to address the fissure.
The surgeon fires the stapler on the middle lobe bronchus, completing the bronchial division. A small chest drain is prepared for insertion, with minimal air leak and blood loss expected.
The surgeon performs the final firing on the horizontal fissure using a robotic surgical system, completing the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.
